blurb: Upright annual frosted with white-margined leaves and bracts late season.
seasons: Green early; white-edged variegated foliage & bracts late summer–fall
toxic: Milky sap irritates skin/eyes — wear gloves; caution with kids/pets

care.hardiness: A warm-season annual — it germinates, grows, blooms, and sets seed in one season and is killed outright by the first hard frost. There is no overwintering (unless it reseeds). Its native range spans the central and southern plains of North America including Colorado, so it is perfectly adapted to Front Range summers. USDA hardiness zones are not applicable for annuals.
care.sun: Full sun is best — 6+ hours of direct light produces the most robust, upright plants with the brightest white-variegated foliage. It tolerates some afternoon shade but can become floppy and less showy with too little sun. On the dry, high-altitude Front Range, full sun suits it well.
care.soil: Thrives in average to poor, well-drained soil — it is native to dry prairies and tolerates rocky, sandy, and clay-loam textures including the alkaline soils common in Front Range yards. Rich, moist, heavily amended soil makes it floppy. No fertilizer or compost needed at planting; lean ground is ideal. Good drainage is the main requirement.
care.water: Low to moderate once established. Water transplants and seedlings regularly until rooted in; after that, water deeply every 7–10 days in summer heat. It handles the periodic dry spells typical of the Front Range without drama. Overwatering leads to root rot in heavy soils — err on the side of dry.
care.spacing: Space 6–12 in. apart in the garden; plants grow 1–3 ft tall and about 1–2 ft wide with a single upright stem. For a bold colony effect, sow in drifts. In naturalized stands it self-thins as the season progresses.
care.sow: Direct-sow outdoors in early to mid-spring once the soil is workable and frosts are mostly done — around late April to mid-May in Boulder. Alternatively, start indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost (roughly early April). It taproots and dislikes transplanting, so direct sowing is preferable. Rake seed shallowly into the soil surface or press it in — it may need some light to germinate.
care.depth: Sow shallowly, about ¼ in. deep (or simply pressed into the soil surface). Germination is erratic and takes 10–14 days at soil temperatures of 60–75°F. Sow more seed than you think you need; expect some gaps. Thin to 6–12 in. once seedlings are established.
care.bloom: Small greenish-yellow true flowers appear mid-summer, but the ornamental show — the striking white-margined leaves and white-bracted upper foliage — begins in July and builds through August and September until frost kills the plant. The whole plant becomes increasingly frosted-white as the season advances.
care.feeding: None needed. This is a prairie plant of lean, poor soils; fertilizing produces lush leafy growth and weakens the upright habit. Skip compost and fertilizer entirely for the best form.
care.maintenance: Essentially none during the season. If self-seeding is not wanted, cut plants back before seed matures in fall. Otherwise, let the pods ripen and shatter for next year's colony. Pull or compost spent plants after the first killing frost.
care.selfsow: Prolific self-sower — it naturalizes readily in open, disturbed soil and returns year after year from seed. On the Front Range this is a feature, not a problem; volunteers are easy to pull or thin in spring. If you want to limit the spread, deadhead or cut back before seed matures. It is not CO-listed as a noxious weed.
care.troubles: Generally trouble-free. Aphids occasionally cluster on stems — knock off with a strong water spray. No serious diseases in dry, well-drained conditions. The most important caution is the milky sap: all euphorbia sap is a skin and eye irritant that can cause contact dermatitis; it is toxic if ingested and can irritate mucous membranes. Always wear gloves and eye protection when cutting or pulling plants, and wash hands thoroughly afterward. Keep children and pets from chewing stems. The honey from bees visiting this plant can carry trace irritants, though this is rarely a practical concern.
care.harvest: Collect seed in late summer when pods turn tan and begin to dry, just before they split open explosively. Dry pods in a paper bag indoors, then shake free the dark seeds and store cool and dry. Germination does not require any pretreatment.
care.companions: Other lean-soil, full-sun Front Range annuals and prairie plants: cosmos, California poppy, native sunflowers, blanketflower, and black-eyed Susan. The white-and-green foliage makes a striking contrast to bold red or orange flowers.
care.planting: Direct sowing in place is strongly preferred — the plant develops a taproot early and resents transplanting. Sow outdoors in late April to mid-May in Boulder once the soil is workable and most frosts are past, pressing seeds into the soil surface or just barely covering them. An alternative is a fall sow (October) directly on the ground, letting seeds overwinter naturally and germinate the following spring in their own time. If starting indoors, use individual peat or coir pots you can plant out without disturbing the taproot; set out after mid-May once night temperatures stay above 40°F. In containers, use a gritty, lean potting mix in a pot at least 10–12 in deep to accommodate the taproot. Pots dry out faster than garden beds so check moisture every day or two in hot weather. This is an annual — container plants will simply finish at the first hard frost (early October on the Front Range); no overwintering needed.
care.propagation: Seed is the only method — this annual does not divide, does not spread by runners, and forms no persistent root structure. Collect seed in late summer when pods turn tan and just before they split (see the harvest notes above). Cold-moist stratification is not needed; dry-stored seed germinates readily the following season. The plant's prolific self-sowing is its natural propagation strategy: allow a few plants to ripen and shatter their seeds in place each fall and a new colony will emerge the next spring with no effort. Handle seed and plants with gloves — the milky sap in the stems is a skin and eye irritant.
